vrindavan_places.name: Banke Bihari Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Banke Bihari Temple, Raman Reti, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: The most visited temple in Vrindavan, dedicated to Lord Krishna in his playful 'Banke Bihari' form. The black marble deity is believed to have been discovered by Swami Haridas in Nidhivan. The temple follows a unique tradition — the curtain before the deity is periodically opened and closed so devotees don't get lost in the enchanting gaze of the Lord.
vrindavan_places.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (07:45–09:00) for less crowded darshan

vrindavan_places.name: Prem Mandir
vrindavan_places.address: Prem Mandir, Chatikara Road, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A magnificent white marble temple inaugurated in 2012 by Jagadguru Kripalu Maharaj. The temple's stunning architecture depicts scenes from Radha-Krishna and Sita-Ram leelas through intricate carvings. The evening laser light and fountain show transforms the temple into a breathtaking spectacle of colours and devotional music.
vrindavan_places.best_time_to_visit: Evening for the light and fountain show (19:00)

vrindavan_places.name: ISKCON Krishna Balaram Temple
vrindavan_places.address: ISKCON Temple, Bhaktivedanta Swami Marg, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: The global headquarters of ISKCON in Vrindavan, founded by A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada in 1975. The white marble temple houses beautiful deities of Krishna-Balaram, Radha-Shyamasundar, and Gaura-Nitai. The temple complex includes Prabhupada's samadhi, a guest house, and the famous Govinda's restaurant serving pure vegetarian prasadam.
vrindavan_places.best_time_to_visit: Morning Mangala Aarti (04:30) or evening aarti

vrindavan_places.name: Radha Vallabh Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Radha Vallabh Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: One of the most important temples of the Pushti Marg tradition, established in 1585 by Goswami Hit Harivansh. Unique among Vrindavan temples, only Radha's crown is placed beside the deity — there is no separate Radha idol, signifying her inseparable union with Krishna. The temple's austere beauty and deep devotional atmosphere set it apart.

vrindavan_places.name: Radha Raman Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Radha Raman Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: One of Vrindavan's most revered temples, housing a self-manifested deity of Lord Krishna that appeared from a sacred Shaligram stone in 1542. Established by Gopal Bhatta Goswami, the temple preserves unbroken worship traditions spanning nearly 500 years. The original sacred fire lit by Gopal Bhatta still burns in the temple kitchen.

vrindavan_places.name: Madan Mohan Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Madan Mohan Temple, near Keshi Ghat, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: One of the oldest temples in Vrindavan, perched atop a hill near Keshi Ghat overlooking the Yamuna. Originally built by Kapur Ram Das in the early 16th century and later expanded by the Rajput king Man Singh I of Amber. The original deity was moved to Jaipur during Aurangzeb's reign; a replica now graces the temple.

vrindavan_places.name: Shahji Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Shahji Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A beautiful temple built in 1876 by Shah Kundan Lal of Lucknow. Famous for its 15 exquisitely carved white marble columns with spiralling floral designs — considered among the finest marble work in North India. The Basanti Kamra (Spring Room) features stunning Belgian glass chandeliers and paintings.

vrindavan_places.name: Rangji Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Rangji Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A unique temple blending South Indian Dravidian, North Indian Rajput, and Mughal architectural styles. Built in 1851 by Seth Laxmichand of Hyderabad, the temple features a 50-foot tall gopuram, golden dhwaja stambh, and a man-made lake. Dedicated to Lord Ranganatha (Vishnu), it is the only Dravidian-style temple in Vrindavan.

vrindavan_places.name: Nidhivan
vrindavan_places.address: Nidhivan, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: One of the most mysterious and sacred groves in Vrindavan. According to legend, Radha and Krishna still perform their divine Raas Leela here every night. The grove's tulsi trees grow in pairs with intertwined branches. No living creature — bird, monkey, or insect — stays in Nidhivan after sunset. The temple within houses Radha-Krishna deities dressed fresh each night.

vrindavan_places.name: Seva Kunj
vrindavan_places.address: Seva Kunj, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A sacred garden where Krishna is believed to have performed Seva (service) for Radha by combing her hair and adorning her with flowers. The beautifully maintained grove of Tulsi plants and kadamba trees creates a serene atmosphere. Like Nidhivan, this place is closed after sunset as the divine play is believed to continue each night.

vrindavan_places.name: Keshi Ghat
vrindavan_places.address: Keshi Ghat, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: The most famous ghat in Vrindavan, named after the demon-horse Keshi whom Krishna defeated here. The beautiful stone steps descend to the Yamuna, and the evening aarti is a deeply moving experience. At sunrise, the ghat offers stunning views as the first light falls on the ancient temples and the river shimmers gold.

vrindavan_places.name: Imli Tala
vrindavan_places.address: Imli Tala, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A sacred spot beneath an ancient tamarind tree where Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu (the 15th-century avatar of Krishna) is believed to have meditated and experienced divine ecstasy. The tree is said to be over 5,000 years old, dating back to Krishna's era. Gaudiya Vaishnavas consider this one of the most sacred sites in Vrindavan.

vrindavan_places.name: Pagal Baba Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Pagal Baba Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A multi-storey temple built by a wandering saint fondly called 'Pagal Baba' (mad saint) by locals. The temple sprawls over multiple floors depicting scenes from the epics Ramayana and Mahabharata through life-size dioramas. Each floor is dedicated to different deities and offers panoramic views of Vrindavan from the upper levels.

vrindavan_places.name: Govind Dev Temple Ruins
vrindavan_places.address: Govind Dev Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: The ruins of what was once the grandest and tallest temple in Vrindavan, built by Raja Man Singh I of Amber in 1590. Originally a seven-storey red sandstone masterpiece, it was partially destroyed by Aurangzeb. Even in its reduced four-storey form, the temple's scale and architectural brilliance are awe-inspiring. The original deity was moved to Jaipur for protection.

vrindavan_places.name: Loi Bazaar
vrindavan_places.address: Loi Bazaar, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: The main shopping street of Vrindavan, bustling with shops selling devotional items, colourful deity outfits, bangles, incense, and traditional Braj sweets. Named after the woollen blankets (loi) once sold here, the bazaar is the heartbeat of Vrindavan's daily life and a perfect place to soak in the town's spiritual-commercial culture.

vrindavan_places.name: Govinda’s Restaurant
vrindavan_places.address: Govinda’s Restaurant, ISKCON, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: The famous ISKCON restaurant serving delicious pure vegetarian prasadam. Known for its thalis, pizza, burgers, and sweets — all prepared without onion and garlic in the sattvic tradition. The clean, air-conditioned space offers a welcome respite after temple visits. The food is offered to the deities before serving.

vrindavan_places.name: MVT Restaurant
vrindavan_places.address: MVT Restaurant, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: Located in the Mathura-Vrindavan Trust complex, this popular restaurant offers a wide range of vegetarian dishes. Known for its South Indian dosas, North Indian thalis, and fresh fruit juices. A reliable option for visitors looking for a clean and comfortable dining experience in Vrindavan.

vrindavan_places.name: Vrindavan Chandrodaya Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Vrindavan Chandrodaya Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: An ambitious modern temple project being built as one of the tallest religious structures in the world (planned at 210 metres). The temple will feature a fully recreated Vrindavan forest environment, multimedia exhibitions, and a skywalk. The partially completed structure already impresses with its grand scale and vision.

vrindavan_places.name: Katyayani Peeth
vrindavan_places.address: Katyayani Peeth, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A temple dedicated to Goddess Katyayani, one of the manifestations of Durga. According to the Bhagavata Purana, the gopis of Vrindavan worshipped Katyayani to obtain Krishna as their husband. The temple is especially significant for women devotees who come to pray for marital blessings.

vrindavan_places.name: Radha Damodar Temple
vrindavan_places.address: Radha Damodar Temple, Vrindavan, Uttar Pradesh, India
vrindavan_places.description: A deeply significant Gaudiya Vaishnava temple established by Jiva Goswami in the 16th century. The temple houses Govardhana Shilas (sacred stones from Govardhan Hill) given by Lord Chaitanya himself. Srila Prabhupada, founder of ISKCON, lived and wrote here for six years before travelling to the West. His rooms are preserved as a memorial.
